You blind Pharisees. First wash the inside of the cup and the dish, and then the outside will become clean too. Jesus is giving us a warning here about being hypocrites, about living a life of hypocrisy, a life where everything on the outside looks good. Maybe we've got a high volume or number of good deeds or they're very public, but actually on the inside, we're less than clean. And the first thing is that integrity is about consistency and commitment. Now, I said this last week, but it's still true. I am preaching to myself this month. This is... I've been really convicted by putting these messages together. One of the things that I am in most of my time is a parent. So I have two small children. And I'm one of the ones who makes and enforces the boundaries at home. And there's nothing quite like having children in the house to highlight the areas where you're living as a hypocrite. Like, I don't want to live by the same boundaries as my, you know, 11-year-old daughter. And so the temptation for me is to do the right thing when the kids are looking, and then when they've gone to bed, just do something totally different, right? But that's just not the point of a boundary. If it's good for the kids, it's good for me too. And if I'm going to actually properly lead them, then I need to live by that too. Integrity is about living with consistency, both in private and in public, when people are looking at us and when they're not. If you have a moment this afternoon or sometime this week, make yourself a cup of tea and go and read Matthew, not Matthew, Daniel chapter 1 to 6. Six chapters of Daniel. It's a wonderful story about these guys, Daniel and his friends, who get taken off into exile in a strange land. And they get confronted with loads of challenges to their integrity. Are they going to eat the same food as everybody else? Are they going to bow down and worship the same gods as everybody else? And one after the other, they go through these challenges and they basically say, no, they don't bend under pressure, and they stick to their guns. And it's a wonderful example to us of what it looks like to be living in dark times and to choose God's path, even when those choices get us into big trouble. Hopefully, we're not going to be facing lions or furnaces, but you just never know. Daniel and his friends are a real model to us. And something that we say around here all the time is that if you notice that there's a discrepancy between what I say I believe and what I actually do with my actions, like that's a big alarm bell. We should be paying attention to that. So if we want to live our lives off mute, built on integrity, I think our public and our private lives have to be defined by consistency and commitment. The problem is that even when we do our best, we all mess up. All of us do, which leads us on to our second one, which is about community and confession. Integrity is all about community and confession. So as I say, consistency and commitment, it's a great ideal. The only problem is we're all rubbish at it. Even those of us who find this easier than others will have our bad days. We all have things that we regret. We all mess up. All of us have days and moments of weakness, and all of us have moments when we disappoint ourselves and those around us. The question really becomes, what do we do when we mess up, when we know we've had a lapse of integrity? What do we do? And we live in a culture at the moment which is defined by shame. Like public perception these days is absolutely everything. We think back to those politicians. We still don't like them, do we? Like, oh, you do? Oh, fair play. Okay, we've got one. That's okay. But public perception is everything. And if we mess up, that's a shameful thing. And the problem with a shame culture, there's many problems, but the problem is that when we're ashamed, we hide our brokenness and we isolate ourselves. And it's just the opposite of what Jesus would encourage us to do. Last week, we were talking about Jesus calling us to be the light of the world. And in 1 John 1, verse 7 to 9, we kind of read that idea being unpacked a little bit. It says this, but if we are the light, but if we are living in the light as God is in the light, then we have fellowship with each other and the blood of Jesus, his son, cleanses us from all sin. If we claim we have no sin, we're only fooling ourselves and not living in the truth. But if we confess our sins to him, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and cleanse us from all wickedness. I really appreciate the wording of that. If we claim we have no sin, we're only fooling ourselves and not living in the truth. One of the big things about our society at the moment is cancel culture. Everybody has to live this perfect pedestalled life. And if we don't live that perfect life, we're at risk of being cancelled. I think that's not only unkind in an extreme, it's also totally unrealistic. Like, no one can live up to that standard. I love that language that's used there in 1 John. If we think that we have no sin, we're fooling ourselves and we're not living in the truth. Like, all of us mess up. It's just part of what it is to be human. And the way of Jesus is not the way of shame. So when we mess up, we don't hide. We don't isolate. No, the way of Jesus is the way of confession, which is naming our brokenness to another human being, bringing our mistakes out of the darkness and into the light. Rather than hiding our brokenness and living in shame, integrity is about confessing our brokenness in community. There is just an enormous amount of power in confessing and naming our brokenness and talking to other people about what's going on on the inside. Now, I had an example of this a couple of years ago. I was leading an organisation. I was managing this organisation. And I made a mistake. And it was quite an expensive mistake. And I realised I'd made the mistake. And my instant response was, I need to hide this mistake so that I'm the only one who knows about it. And so I did my best to try and fix this mistake as quickly as I could without anybody else finding out. And I tried my hardest and I failed. And I didn't manage to fix it. And the mistake and the problem and the cost just got bigger and bigger and bigger. And I realised that I needed to go to our directors and say, I've made a mistake. And as you can imagine, I did not enjoy that conversation. I had to go and I had to confess. And I say, look, I'm really sorry. Not only have I made a mistake, not only has it cost us quite a lot of money, I've also hidden it from you. And whilst I've hidden it from you, it's got bigger and bigger and bigger. Now, luckily, blessedly, really, we have a board of Christian directors who showed me mercy. Hallelujah. And that's been my experience of confession in community as well, is that I always think, oh, if I tell that person what I've done, they're just going to hate me. They're going to shun me. They're going to reject me. I've done this a lot. It's never happened. I've always been met with love. Your job in that moment is to say, thank you so much for telling me. That took an enormous amount of courage. Jesus loves you. Let's walk that out together. I don't know, that might be helpful. We're encouraged to learn about living a life of integrity together, which means that when we mess up, when we identify the places of inconsistency in our lives, it's our first response to go into our community and confess it. Speak out. Bring it up and out into the light. We cannot be light on the outside without addressing the darkness on the inside. And the way that we address that darkness is in community and by confession. As I've said, I'm very blessed. There is another encouragement for us to not blend in. It said, don't copy the behavior and customs of this world, but let God transform you into a new person by changing the way you think. And that's how I remember it in my brain. And that word conform that Emily didn't like so much, it got me thinking. We shared some examples earlier on of some politicians, and they failed to attain that level of integrity that we expected of them. And it got me thinking just how much better the world would be if all of us could simply live up to the standard of the society around us, the standard that society expects of us. If we could simply be more tolerant, be more kind, more honest, more patient, more understanding, and more civil, how much better would the world be? Just so much better. But how not only do we fail to live up to that standard, but also that standard of good seems to shift and change. Like, I don't know about you, but it seems that if I, it depends on who I'm speaking to, that definition of good tends to kind of swing left, swing right, be somewhere in the middle. It's a moving feast. Trying to hit that standard of goodness seems to change on a month-by-month basis. And it strikes me in all of this that we're not encouraged to live a life of integrity according to what the world expects. Rather, we are encouraged to live a life transformed by the renewing of our minds. And I think that this is really where integrity is the very way that our faith becomes unignorable. I think this is the way that we be salt and light. Because rather than living our lives aspiring to live up to the world's standard of goodness, which changes all the time and which we always fail to do, we are called to live according to heaven's standard. When we align ourselves with the world's standard of good and bad, at the worst, we fail and we get publicly shamed. At the best, we succeed and we just blend in with everybody else. When we live according to heaven's standard, that's when we get noticed for being different. Rather than blending in with really good values like tolerance, kindness, honesty, patience, understanding, being civil, which are great things, the Christian is called to stand out because we sacrifice ourself for others. We live with radical generosity. We love our enemies and we pray for those who hurt us. We forgive. The true challenge here is when heaven's standard cuts across what I want. When heaven's standard of generosity means that I choose to spend less on myself and more on others. When heaven's standard of forgiveness means that I choose to release those who have hurt me rather than seeking revenge. When heaven's standard of dignity and love means that I choose to avoid pornography when I'm tempted. When heaven's standard of compassion means that I choose to help and hear that person in need when I just have a million other things to do. I think if we wanna take seriously Jesus' call to live as salt and light, I think the journey starts right here by living lives of radical integrity, not by the standard that the world hopes and expects from us, but by the standard of life that Jesus has demonstrated for us.
